evals.name: bare-invocation
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/extract-work-items/SKILL.md fully. You are running the extract-work-items skill.

A user invokes: /extract-work-items (with no arguments)

Follow the skill's 'Initial Setup' section when no parameters are provided.

Save to outputs/:
- response.md: Your exact response to the user

Stop after your initial response. Do NOT spawn any agents. Do NOT read any documents. Do NOT write any files. Do NOT call work-item-next-number.sh.
evals.expected_output: Skill asks helpfully which documents to work with, provides examples of what kinds of sources work, and mentions the option to scan all configured directories. Response is informative and welcoming, not just a single terse question.
expectations: Response offers to scan all configured directories as one option
Response offers the option to specify files manually as an alternative
Response does not proceed to read or list any documents before user answers
Response does not include any candidate list, work item drafts, or extracted content
Response includes examples or context about what kinds of sources can be provided (e.g. files, planning docs, meeting notes)

evals.name: candidate-type-inference
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/extract-work-items/SKILL.md fully. You are running the extract-work-items skill.

The working directory is the plugin root (the directory containing skills/, scripts/, and agents/).

A user invokes: /extract-work-items skills/work/extract-work-items/evals/fixtures/mixed-requirements.md

Follow Steps 1 and 2:
- Step 1: The file exists. Read it fully.
- Step 2: Analyse the document yourself (no need to spawn an actual agent). Present the full candidate list.

The user responds: "all"

For each selected candidate, build the source-derived skeleton (Step 3.1) and present it with the four options (Step 3.2).

The user responds to EACH candidate in turn: "accept as-is"

Stop after building and presenting all skeletons. Do NOT proceed to Step 4. Do NOT call work-item-next-number.sh at any point.

Save to outputs/:
- candidates.md: The exact numbered candidate list presented in Step 2
- skeletons.md: All source-derived skeletons from Step 3.1 (one per candidate, clearly separated)
- behavior.md: For each candidate: (a) its title as presented, (b) the type inferred in Step 3.1, (c) whether a Drafting Notes section was included in the skeleton, (d) whether XXXX was used as the work item number placeholder, (e) whether the Drafting Notes section contains any explanation of why the work item type was chosen, (f) does the Drafting Notes section contain any substantive interpretation notes (beyond the verbatim thin-draft text)?, (g) is there an Assumptions section in the skeleton, and if so, what kinds of interpretations does it contain?
evals.expected_output: Four candidates with correct types (bug, story, spike, epic). Assumptions section (when present) contains scope-changing interpretations. Drafting Notes sections contain meaningful broader interpretations, not explanations of type choice or other mechanical field selections. Open Questions populated where source raises unanswered business questions.
expectations: The dashboard charts stale data item is assigned type 'bug' in its skeleton
The CSV export item is assigned type 'story' in its skeleton
The search options evaluation item is assigned type 'spike' in its skeleton
The auth system modernisation item is assigned type 'epic' in its skeleton
Every skeleton uses XXXX as the work_item_id placeholder (work-item-next-number.sh not called)
The bug skeleton's Requirements section contains reproduction steps and expected/actual behaviour
The spike skeleton's Requirements section contains the research questions, time-box, and exit criterion
The epic skeleton's Requirements section contains the initial story decomposition as a list

The Drafting Notes section does not contain routine type-choice documentation for obvious type assignments (bug and story are unambiguous here); a note explaining a non-obvious inference (e.g. spike vs story when it could be either) is acceptable

The Drafting Notes section does not contain routine priority-mapping boilerplate; a priority note is only acceptable if it explains a genuine scope interpretation tied to urgency language in the source
At least one skeleton's Drafting Notes section contains a substantive interpretation note beyond the verbatim thin-draft text
If an Assumptions section is present in any skeleton, it contains interpretations where the wrong call would change scope or what gets built — not routine field selections

evals.name: thin-draft-verbatim-note
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/extract-work-items/SKILL.md fully. You are running the extract-work-items skill.

The working directory is the plugin root (the directory containing skills/, scripts/, and agents/).

A user invokes: /extract-work-items skills/work/extract-work-items/evals/fixtures/simple-story.md

Follow Steps 1 and 2. Read the file, analyse it yourself, present the candidate list.

The user responds: "all"

For each selected candidate, build the source-derived skeleton (Step 3.1) and present it with the four options (Step 3.2).

The user responds to EACH candidate: "accept as-is"

Apply Step 3.4 (accept as-is — thin draft) for each candidate.

Stop after producing all thin drafts. Do NOT proceed to Step 4. Do NOT call work-item-next-number.sh.

Save to outputs/:
- thin-drafts.md: All thin draft content after Step 3.4 processing (one per candidate, clearly separated)
- behavior.md: (a) How many thin drafts were produced? (b) Does each thin draft contain a Drafting Notes section? (c) Copy the exact text of the Drafting Notes note appended in Step 3.4 for the first thin draft. (d) Does each thin draft contain an Open Questions section? (e) If an Assumptions section is present, what does it contain?
evals.expected_output: Each thin draft has the verbatim Step 3.4 note in a Drafting Notes section (not Assumptions). Open Questions section is present and captures any unanswered business questions raised by the source.
expectations: Each thin draft contains the exact phrase 'Extracted from source documents without interactive enrichment'
Each thin draft contains the exact phrase 'Acceptance criteria, dependencies, and type may need refinement before promoting from `draft` to `ready`'
work-item-next-number.sh was NOT called at any point during this process
Each thin draft uses XXXX as the work_item_id placeholder
Each thin draft contains an Open Questions section
Each thin draft has a section headed 'Drafting Notes' (not 'Assumptions') that contains the verbatim non-enrichment note

evals.name: structural-content-excluded
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/extract-work-items/SKILL.md fully. You are running the extract-work-items skill.

The working directory is the plugin root (the directory containing skills/, scripts/, and agents/).

A user invokes: /extract-work-items skills/work/extract-work-items/evals/fixtures/structural-content.md

Follow Steps 1 and 2. Read the file, analyse it yourself (no need to spawn an actual agent), and present the candidate list.

The user responds: "none"

Exit cleanly as the skill instructs.

Save to outputs/:
- candidates.md: The exact numbered candidate list you presented to the user (or a statement that no actionable items were found)
- behavior.md: (a) How many candidates did you present? (b) List the exact title/description of each candidate you identified. (c) Confirm whether any of these were included as candidates: the table of contents entries (Introduction, Current Issues, Proposed Solutions, Implementation Plan, Next Steps), the navigation items (Overview, Architecture, API Reference, Changelog), or the 'Next Steps: Discuss with team' line.
evals.expected_output: Only the notification preferences requirement is presented as a candidate (1 item). Table of contents entries, navigation headings, 'Implementation Plan: TBD', and 'Next Steps: Discuss with team' are NOT candidates.
expectations: Candidate list presents at most 2 actionable items (notification preferences is the clear one)
Table of contents entries (Introduction, Current Issues, Proposed Solutions, Implementation Plan, Next Steps) are NOT listed as candidates
Navigation items (Overview, Architecture, API Reference, Changelog) are NOT listed as candidates
The 'Next Steps: Discuss with team' line is NOT listed as a candidate
The 'Implementation Plan: TBD based on engineering estimates' line is NOT listed as a candidate
The notification preferences requirement IS identified as a candidate

evals.name: non-existent-path
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/extract-work-items/SKILL.md fully. You are running the extract-work-items skill.

The working directory is the plugin root (the directory containing skills/, scripts/, and agents/).

A user invokes: /extract-work-items meta/research/does-not-exist-2026-04-20.md

Follow Step 1 of the skill. Handle the case where the specified file does not exist.

Save to outputs/:
- response.md: Your exact response to the user
- behavior.md: (a) Did you report the missing file path to the user? (yes/no) (b) Did you proceed with an empty candidate list or pretend the file was found? (yes/no) (c) What action did you ask the user to take?

Stop after your response about the missing file. Do NOT spawn any agents. Do NOT proceed to Step 2.
evals.expected_output: Skill names the missing path explicitly, does NOT silently skip it or proceed with an empty list, and asks the user to correct or remove the path before continuing.
expectations: Response explicitly names the missing path 'meta/research/does-not-exist-2026-04-20.md'
Response states the file does not exist (uses language like 'not found', 'does not exist', or 'missing')
Response asks the user to correct or remove the path
Response does NOT present an empty candidate list as if the file was found
Response does NOT proceed to Step 2 or spawn any agents

evals.name: source-faithfulness-assumptions
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/extract-work-items/SKILL.md fully. You are running the extract-work-items skill.

The working directory is the plugin root (the directory containing skills/, scripts/, and agents/).

A user invokes: /extract-work-items skills/work/extract-work-items/evals/fixtures/sparse-feature.md

Follow Steps 1 and 2. Read the file, analyse it yourself, present the candidate(s).

The user responds: "1" (selects the first candidate)

Build the source-derived skeleton (Step 3.1). The source is intentionally sparse — it says very little.

Present the skeleton with the four options (Step 3.2).

The user responds: "accept as-is"

Apply Step 3.4. Stop here. Do NOT call work-item-next-number.sh.

Save to outputs/:
- skeleton.md: The source-derived skeleton from Step 3.1
- thin-draft.md: The final thin draft after Step 3.4
- behavior.md: (a) Does the skeleton include acceptance criteria or specific requirements NOT stated in the source? (b) Are scope-changing business interpretations flagged in Assumptions (a separate section from Drafting Notes)? (c) Are broader drafting interpretations captured in Drafting Notes? (d) Are genuine unknowns placed in Open Questions? (e) Does the Drafting Notes section contain any explanation of why the work item type was chosen, or why a priority level was selected? (f) Does the Summary accurately reflect only what the source says? (g) If an Assumptions section is present, do the assumptions reflect interpretations where the wrong call would change scope?
evals.expected_output: Source faithfully represented. Scope-changing interpretations in Assumptions section (if any were made). Broader drafting notes in Drafting Notes. Open Questions captures what the sparse source leaves unanswered. Summary reflects only what the source states.
expectations: The skeleton's Summary reflects only what the source document actually states
Any acceptance criteria or specific requirements NOT in the source are flagged via Assumptions, Drafting Notes, or Open Questions
The Drafting Notes section captures broader drafting interpretations (scope boundaries, who stakeholders are, vague term meanings) rather than field-choice reasoning
The Drafting Notes section does NOT explain why the work item type was chosen or why a priority level was selected
Open Questions contains at least one question about a genuine business unknown raised by the sparse source
The thin draft carries the verbatim non-enrichment note from Step 3.4 in the Drafting Notes section
If an Assumptions section is present, it contains interpretations where the wrong call would change what gets built — not routine field selections

evals.name: post-enrichment-approval-clarity
evals.prompt:
Read skills/work/extract-work-items/SKILL.md fully. You are running the extract-work-items skill.

The working directory is the plugin root (the directory containing skills/, scripts/, and agents/).

A user invokes: /extract-work-items skills/work/extract-work-items/evals/fixtures/sparse-feature.md

Follow Steps 1 and 2. Read the file, analyse it yourself, present candidate(s).

The user responds: "1"

Build the source-derived skeleton (Step 3.1) and present with options (Step 3.2).

The user responds: "enrich"

Begin enrichment (Step 3.3):
- Ask 1-3 focused business-context questions tailored to this candidate.
- The user answers: "The search team needs this to understand if the current Elasticsearch setup is worth keeping. Affects the 2 devs who maintain it plus product who relies on search quality. Done = written report with a go/no-go recommendation. Time-box 3 days."
- For the web research step: simulate the results yourself without spawning an actual agent. Assume research finds: Elasticsearch has high operational complexity vs Typesense/Meilisearch which are simpler alternatives.
- Produce an enriched draft and present it to the user.

Stop after presenting the enriched draft and your prompt to the user.

Do NOT call work-item-next-number.sh. Do NOT write any files.

Save to outputs/:
- enriched-draft.md: The full enriched draft you present
- post-enrichment-prompt.md: The EXACT words you show the user AFTER presenting the enriched draft — the message asking them what to do next
- behavior.md: (a) Is 'approve' an explicit numbered option in the post-enrichment prompt? (b) Is revision explicit? (c) Is 'skip' a numbered option? (d) Is 'accept as-is' a numbered option? (e) Does the enriched draft Drafting Notes section contain only business-context interpretations (not type/priority reasoning)? (f) Does the enriched draft contain testable acceptance criteria? (g) Does the enriched draft have an Assumptions section, and if so, do the assumptions reflect scope-changing interpretations (e.g. interpreting vague source terms in ways that affect what gets built)?
evals.expected_output: Post-enrichment prompt has four numbered options. Enriched draft has testable ACs. If scope-changing interpretations were made, they appear in the Assumptions section. Drafting Notes contains broader interpretations only — no field-choice reasoning.
expectations: Post-enrichment prompt explicitly names 'approve' (or 'approved') as a numbered option
Post-enrichment prompt makes clear that the user can provide revision instructions to update the draft
Post-enrichment prompt mentions 'skip' as a numbered option
Post-enrichment prompt mentions 'accept as-is' as a numbered option
Enriched draft contains acceptance criteria that are specific and testable
Enriched draft reflects the Q&A answers (search team, 2 devs, go/no-go recommendation, 3-day time-box)
The enriched draft's Drafting Notes section does not contain explanations of why the work item type or priority was chosen
If an Assumptions section is present in the enriched draft, it contains scope-changing interpretations (not the same content that is already reflected in Requirements or Acceptance Criteria)
